h1Title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD) is the most common potentially fatal hereditary disorder in adults of Northern European descent, caused by mutations in the SERPINA1 gene that reduce or alter the serum protease inhibitor alpha-1 antitrypsin. With approximately 1 in 2,500 to 1 in 5,000 individuals of European ancestry carrying the severe ZZ genotype, an estimated 100,000 to 200,000 Americans are affected, though 85-90% remain undiagnosed. Low AAT levels allow unchecked neutrophil elastase activity to destroy lung parenchyma, causing early-onset emphysema often by age 40-50 in smokers. The misfolded Z-protein also polymerizes in hepatocytes, causing liver disease ranging from neonatal cholestasis to cirrhosis. Intravenous AAT augmentation therapy slows emphysema progression in the PiZZ genotype.

keyStats.prevalence: Severe ZZ genotype in approximately 1 in 2,500 to 1 in 5,000 individuals of Northern European ancestry; estimated 3.4 million individuals globally carry two deficiency alleles (de Serres 2002)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in individuals of Northern and Western European descent; rare in East Asian populations; affects men and women equally
keyStats.avgAge: Pulmonary symptoms typically appear at age 30-50 in smokers (10-15 years earlier than in non-smokers); liver disease may present in neonates or not until adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100,000-200,000 Americans with severe AATD; 3.4 million globally with severe genotypes; 85-90% undiagnosed worldwide

definition: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (ICD-10: E88.01) is an autosomal codominant genetic disorder caused by mutations in the SERPINA1 gene encoding the serine protease inhibitor alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT). Normal AAT production is 20-48 µmol/L; the common M allele produces normal protein; the Z allele (Glu342Lys substitution) causes AAT to misfold and polymerize within hepatocytes, reducing serum levels to approximately 15-20% of normal (3-7 µmol/L). The consequence is dual: in the lung, unopposed neutrophil elastase degrades alveolar elastin, causing basal-predominant panacinar emphysema; in the liver, polymer accumulation in hepatocytes causes hepatocellular injury, fibrosis, and cirrhosis. The S allele (Glu264Val) produces protein at 60% of normal levels; SS genotype rarely causes lung disease alone. SZ compound heterozygotes produce AAT at 35-40% of normal and carry intermediate risk. The null genotype (PI*QQ) produces no protein and has the highest emphysema risk but no liver disease. PiZZ is the prototypic severe genotype, managed by pulmonologists, hepatologists, and clinical geneticists. Augmentation therapy (IV pooled human AAT) is the only approved disease-modifying therapy for pulmonary disease.

typesClassification.description: Two Z alleles; serum AAT approximately 3-7 µmol/L. Highest risk for panacinar emphysema (particularly in smokers), liver disease (neonatal cholestasis, cirrhosis), and panniculitis. The target of AAT augmentation therapy.
One S and one Z allele; serum AAT 35-40% of normal. Intermediate emphysema risk — substantially elevated in smokers and those with occupational dust exposure. Liver disease less common than PiZZ.

One M (normal) and one Z allele; serum AAT 55-65% of normal. Lung disease risk is mildly elevated in smokers; primary clinical importance is genetic counseling (each child of two MZ parents has a 25% chance of PiZZ).

Two null alleles produce no circulating AAT protein; serum levels undetectable. Highest emphysema risk of all genotypes; no liver disease because there is no misfolded protein accumulating in hepatocytes.

typesClassification.type: Rare Z-related variants (PI*ZZ with liver disease)
typesClassification.description: Some Z allele carriers develop progressive liver fibrosis through polymer accumulation-driven hepatocyte injury. The mechanism is gain-of-function hepatotoxicity from intracellular protein aggregation, distinct from the loss-of-function emphysema mechanism.

primarySymptoms: Progressive exertional breathlessness developing from age 30-50 in smokers or 45-60 in non-smokers, from emphysematous destruction of lower-lobe alveoli that reduces gas exchange surface area.
Chronic cough with or without sputum production — a symptom shared with COPD from smoking, but occurring at a younger age and with disproportionate hyperinflation on chest imaging.
Wheezing and bronchospasm, present in approximately 30% of AATD patients from concomitant airway inflammation and bronchial hyperreactivity.
Recurrent lower respiratory tract infections (bronchitis, pneumonia) with prolonged recovery, reflecting impaired airway defense from reduced AAT-mediated neutrophil elastase inhibition.
Jaundice, hepatomegaly, and pruritus in neonates with homozygous AATD presenting as neonatal cholestasis; resolves spontaneously in 80-90% of affected infants.
Signs of chronic liver disease in adults (jaundice, spider angiomata, splenomegaly, ascites) when hepatic AAT polymer accumulation has progressed to fibrosis or cirrhosis.
Tender erythematous skin nodules on the trunk and proximal extremities characteristic of AATD-associated panniculitis — an under-recognized feature of severe deficiency.
Systemic vasculitis (predominantly ANCA-associated) has a two- to threefold higher prevalence in AATD, presenting with renal impairment, sinusitis, or pulmonary hemorrhage.
Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance as lung hyperinflation worsens, increasing the work of breathing and reducing cardiac preload.
Peripheral edema and orthopnea in late-stage lung or liver disease when pulmonary hypertension or portal hypertension develop.
earlyWarningSigns: Early-onset emphysema (under age 45) without a significant smoking history — a diagnosis of emphysema or COPD in a non-smoker or light smoker should trigger AATD testing in every case.
Persistent basilar hyperinflation on chest X-ray in a young adult — panacinar emphysema preferentially destroys the lung bases, unlike smoking-related COPD which favors the upper lobes.
Unexplained liver enzyme elevation (AST, ALT, GGT) in a neonate, child, or young adult without other obvious cause — screen for AATD.
Family history of early emphysema, liver cirrhosis without alcohol use, or known AATD diagnosis — all first-degree relatives should be offered testing.
Repeated respiratory infections in a young adult with airflow limitation on spirometry — consider AATD before attributing entirely to asthma.
emergencySigns: Acute severe breathlessness with SpO2 below 88% in a known AATD patient — represents acute-on-chronic exacerbation or pneumothorax; pneumothorax is more common in emphysematous AATD lungs.
Hematemesis or signs of variceal bleeding in a patient with known or suspected AATD-related cirrhosis — requires urgent resuscitation and endoscopy.
Acute skin necrosis or rapidly spreading ulcerating nodules in AATD-related panniculitis — may require urgent surgical debridement and dapsone or colchicine therapy.
Acute confusion and asterixis in AATD cirrhosis — signals hepatic encephalopathy requiring immediate ammonia reduction and liver support.

causes.description: The most common severe genotype globally. The Glu342Lys point mutation in exon 5 of SERPINA1 causes AAT to misfold and form intracellular polymer chains within hepatocytes, reducing secreted protein to 15-20% of normal. Inherited in an autosomal codominant pattern; both parents must carry at least one Z allele to produce a ZZ child.

One S allele (Glu264Val, secreted at 60% of normal) and one Z allele (secreted at 15-20% of normal) yield combined serum AAT at 35-40% of normal. This is sufficient to cause significant emphysema risk in smokers and mild liver disease risk, though substantially lower than PiZZ.

Over 30 null alleles produce no circulating AAT. Serum AAT is undetectable in null-null homozygotes. Maximum emphysema risk because there is no AAT to inhibit elastase. No liver disease because there is no protein to accumulate intracellularly.

Smoking is the dominant modifier of AATD lung phenotype. Cigarette smoke oxidizes the active site methionine of AAT, inactivating residual protein, and directly recruits neutrophils to the lung. PiZZ smokers develop clinically significant emphysema on average 10-15 years earlier than PiZZ non-smokers.

Grain dust, coal dust, isocyanates, and welding fumes accelerate emphysema progression in AATD, likely through similar mechanisms of oxidative inactivation of residual AAT and direct neutrophil recruitment. Occupational history is critical when counseling affected individuals.

riskFactors.description: The Z allele originated in Scandinavia and has highest prevalence in populations of Northern European descent. PiZZ prevalence is approximately 1 in 2,500 in Sweden; 1 in 5,000 across the US. Very rare in East Asian and sub-Saharan African populations.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of AATD, early emphysema, or unexplained liver cirrhosis
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of PiZZ individuals have a 25-50% probability of carrying at least one Z allele. ATS/ERS guidelines recommend testing all first-degree relatives of any confirmed AATD case.

The single most important modifiable risk factor for pulmonary disease. Smoking accelerates FEV1 decline by 50-100 mL/year in PiZZ patients vs. 25-50 mL/year in non-smokers with AATD. Cessation at any point substantially slows lung function loss.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational exposures to dust, fumes, or organic solvents
riskFactors.description: Coal miners, grain workers, and welders with AATD face significantly accelerated emphysema progression. Occupational health surveillance and exposure reduction are essential components of management.
Approximately 10-15% of PiZZ neonates develop clinically evident neonatal cholestasis; a minority progress to juvenile cirrhosis. Liver involvement in adulthood may occur without a neonatal history.

Co-existing alcohol use substantially accelerates hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis in PiZZ individuals with AATD-related liver disease. Alcohol cessation is the most impactful modifiable hepatic risk factor.
HIV-related immune dysregulation and antiretroviral-associated oxidative stress may accelerate emphysema in AATD. Early antiretroviral therapy reduces this risk by normalizing immune activation.

affectedDemographics: Individuals of Northern and Western European descent carry the highest prevalence; Sweden and Denmark have prevalence of PiZZ approaching 1 in 1,600 in some regions.
Hispanic populations in the US carry a lower prevalence of the Z allele but a higher prevalence of the S allele; SZ compound heterozygosity is relatively more common than in non-Hispanic whites.
Men and women are equally affected at the genetic level, but women with AATD historically had better lung function preservation — a difference that has narrowed with the convergence of smoking rates.
An estimated 85-90% of all AATD individuals worldwide remain undiagnosed, reflecting the condition's rarity, its mimicry of common COPD, and historically low awareness among clinicians.
Children of two MZ parents or one ZZ and one MZ parent have a 25-50% risk of PiZZ genotype; targeted genetic testing is recommended in these families.
diagnosisOverview: The American Thoracic Society (ATS) and European Respiratory Society (ERS) 2003 consensus statement recommends testing all patients with early-onset emphysema, COPD without risk factors, or unexplained liver disease. Diagnosis begins with serum AAT quantification by immunologic assay (nephelometry or immunoturbidimetry); a normal result (above 20 µmol/L or above 100 mg/dL depending on the assay) largely excludes clinically significant AATD, though a normal level during acute inflammation may not reflect true genotype. A low serum level triggers phenotyping by isoelectric focusing (IEF), which separates the distinct migration bands of the M, S, and Z proteins. Definitive genotyping is performed by targeted allele-specific PCR for the Z and S mutations, with sequencing for rare or null alleles. Spirometry assesses airflow limitation; diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO) is characteristically reduced in emphysema. CT thorax with inspiratory and expiratory views quantifies emphysema distribution and severity — basal (lower-lobe) panacinar emphysema is characteristic of AATD, contrasting with the upper-lobe paraseptal distribution of usual smoking-related COPD. Liver function tests, alpha-fetoprotein, and liver ultrasound screen for hepatic involvement; percutaneous liver biopsy with PAS-diastase staining confirms AAT polymer inclusions in PAS-D-positive globules within periportal hepatocytes. Cascade testing of first-degree relatives follows any confirmed diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies circulating AAT; levels below 11 µmol/L (57 mg/dL) indicate severe deficiency and warrant genotyping
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. AAT is an acute-phase reactant, so levels may be falsely elevated during infection or inflammation — repeat in 4-6 weeks if elevated during acute illness. Results within 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the protein isoforms present (M, S, Z bands) and detects common phenotypes including MZ, SS, SZ, ZZ
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw processed in a specialty laboratory. Z band appears as a distinctive band on IEF gel. Results within 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms Z, S, or null alleles; identifies rare mutations missed by phenotyping
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; can also be done by buccal swab in some direct-to-consumer panels. Definitive for carrier status, prenatal counseling, and clinical trial eligibility. Results within 2-3 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects airflow obstruction (FEV1/FVC below 0.70 post-bronchodilator) and quantifies severity per GOLD classification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blow into a mouthpiece after a deep breath, before and after a bronchodilator inhaler. Takes 20-30 minutes. Reduced FEV1 and DLCO precede CT changes.

diagnosticTests.test: High-resolution CT thorax with emphysema quantification
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates basal-predominant panacinar emphysema; quantitative CT scores predict progression and augmentation therapy response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15-minute scan lying still; no contrast needed for emphysema assessment. Inspiratory and expiratory images obtained. Reports note lung density histogram and Perc15 (15th percentile density) as quantitative emphysema marker.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and abdominal ultrasound
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for AATD-related hepatic involvement; elevated GGT often the first signal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for LFTs; 20-30 minute ultrasound of the abdomen. Ultrasound detects echogenicity change, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and portal hypertension features.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms AAT polymer accumulation in periportal hepatocytes; grades fibrosis stage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound-guided percutaneous needle biopsy under local anesthesia; day case procedure. PAS-D-positive globules within hepatocytes are diagnostic of AATD liver disease. Risk of bleeding is approximately 0.5%.

treatmentOverview: Management of AATD addresses both the pulmonary and hepatic manifestations and their underlying mechanisms. Smoking cessation is the single most impactful intervention for lung disease — PiZZ smokers who quit show FEV1 preservation approaching that of non-smokers within 5 years. Standard COPD therapies apply to symptomatic AATD lung disease: long-acting beta-agonists (LABA), long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMA), and inhaled corticosteroids reduce exacerbations and improve quality of life. Pulmonary rehabilitation increases exercise capacity and reduces hospitalization rates. Augmentation therapy with intravenous pooled human AAT (Prolastin-C, Zemaira, Glassia, Aralast) is the only disease-specific treatment, approved in the US, Canada, and Europe for PiZZ patients with FEV1 30-65% predicted and confirmed emphysema. The standard dose is 60 mg/kg IV weekly; alternative regimens of 120 mg/kg biweekly are being studied. The RAPID trial (2015, Lancet) demonstrated that augmentation therapy significantly slows CT-measured lung density loss (−1.45 g/L/year less density loss vs. placebo, p = 0.006). However, augmentation does not reverse established emphysema and is not indicated for liver disease. For AATD liver disease, treatment mirrors that for other chronic liver diseases: alcohol abstinence, management of portal hypertension, and liver transplantation for end-stage disease. Liver transplantation is curative for the hepatic phenotype and simultaneously restores normal AAT production. Lung transplantation is offered for FEV1 below 20% predicted with rapid decline. Lung volume reduction surgery can benefit carefully selected AATD patients with heterogeneous emphysema. The GOLD 2024 guidelines specifically address AATD as a COPD phenotype requiring augmentation in eligible patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous AAT augmentation therapy (Prolastin-C, Zemaira, Glassia) 60 mg/kg/week
medicalTreatments.description: Replaces circulating AAT via pooled human plasma-derived protein; maintains serum AAT above the protective threshold of 11 µmol/L. Indicated for PiZZ or PiNull patients with FEV1 30-65% predicted who have documented emphysema. Infused over 15-30 minutes weekly at home or in an infusion center.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: RAPID trial (2015): slows CT lung density loss by 34% relative to placebo; no significant effect on FEV1 decline in 24 months; long-term extension showed mortality benefit

medicalTreatments.name: Bronchodilator therapy (LABA/LAMA combinations)
medicalTreatments.description: Tiotropium (LAMA), salmeterol or formoterol (LABA), and combination devices (umeclidinium/vilanterol, glycopyrrolate/formoterol) reduce air trapping, improve FEV1 by 100-200 mL, and lower exacerbation rates per GOLD COPD guidelines applied to AATD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: LABA/LAMA combinations reduce moderate-to-severe exacerbations by 25-30% vs. LAMA alone in GOLD stage C-D COPD

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) in combination inhalers
medicalTreatments.description: Added to LABA/LAMA in patients with frequent exacerbations or concomitant asthmatic features. GOLD 2024 recommends triple therapy (ICS/LABA/LAMA) in eosinophil count above 300/µL or with high exacerbation burden.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ICS/LABA reduces exacerbations by 25-30% in GOLD stage D; risk of pneumonia is elevated with ICS in severe emphysema

medicalTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation (exercise training, 8-12 week program)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured exercise and education program for patients with FEV1 below 60% predicted or MRC dyspnea scale above grade 2. Improves 6-minute walk distance by 50-80 m, reduces hospitalization rates, and improves health-related quality of life.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane review: reduces hospital admissions by 38% and all-cause mortality in COPD at 1 year; benefits last 12-18 months post-program

medicalTreatments.name: Dapsone (100 mg/day) for AATD-associated panniculitis
medicalTreatments.description: A neutrophil-directed anti-inflammatory that reduces the skin nodule burden of AATD panniculitis. Colchicine (0.6 mg twice daily) is an alternative. Doxycycline 100 mg/day has been used for mild disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Retrospective series: 60-70% response rate for new lesion suppression; established nodules may liquefy and require surgical drainage

medicalTreatments.name: Liver transplantation
medicalTreatments.description: Curative treatment for end-stage AATD liver disease; donor liver produces normal AAT, restoring serum levels to normal. Simultaneous lung transplantation is possible for combined end-stage lung and liver disease. 5-year post-transplant survival exceeds 80% in most centers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recurrence of AATD liver disease after transplant does not occur with recipient liver; lung disease continues unless lung is also transplanted

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation (single or bilateral)
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for AATD emphysema with FEV1 below 20% predicted, rapid decline, or pulmonary hypertension not amenable to medical therapy. Bilateral lung transplant preferred over single-lung because of the risk of native lung hyperinflation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival 50-60%; median survival after bilateral lung transplant for AATD approximately 5.5 years (ISHLT 2022 registry data)

surgicalOptions.name: Lung volume reduction surgery (LVRS) or endobronchial valve placement
surgicalOptions.description: Selectively removes or collapses the most destroyed lung regions to reduce hyperinflation and improve mechanics in heterogeneous emphysema. Benefit in AATD is more modest than in non-AATD COPD because emphysema is diffuse and basal-predominant.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Selected patients (heterogeneous, upper-lobe predominant) may gain 150-200 mL FEV1 and improved 6-minute walk; evidence in AATD specifically is limited to case series

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation pharmacotherapy (varenicline, nicotine replacement)
alternativeTreatments.description: Evidence-based cessation aids are the single most important intervention in AATD; varenicline roughly triples 12-month abstinence rates vs. unassisted cessation in general COPD trials and is appropriate for all AATD patients who smoke.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with confirmed AATD should be seen by a pulmonologist experienced in COPD/AATD for lung function surveillance and augmentation therapy decisions. A hepatologist should evaluate any biochemical liver abnormality. Genetic counseling is essential for reproductive planning and cascade family testing.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a pulmonologist affiliated with an Alpha-1 Foundation-recognized care center or a National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute Rare Disease Clinical Research Network site. These centers have experience with augmentation infusion, CT-based lung density tracking, and clinical trial access. Hepatologists specializing in metabolic liver disease are ideal for the hepatic phenotype.
hospitalCriteria: Alpha-1 Foundation-recognized center for augmentation therapy and clinical trial access
Pulmonary function laboratory with quantitative CT emphysema measurement capability
Hepatology service experienced in metabolic liver disease and liver biopsy
Genetic counseling service for SERPINA1 cascade testing
Lung and liver transplant programs for end-stage disease
keyFacilities: AATD specialist pulmonology and hepatology clinics
NHLBI Rare Disease Clinical Research Network sites
Lung transplant centers with AATD experience
Genetic counseling centers for SERPINA1 testing
preventionStrategies: Never smoke and stop immediately if currently smoking — the most impactful single action for any person with AATD or a family history of AATD.
Avoid occupational exposure to dusts, fumes, grain, coal, and isocyanates; wear appropriate respiratory protective equipment in all dusty work environments.
Vaccinate against influenza annually and pneumococcal disease (PCV15/PPSV23 per CDC adult schedule) to reduce respiratory infections that accelerate lung damage.
All first-degree relatives of a confirmed AATD patient should undergo serum AAT testing and genotyping to identify affected individuals before symptoms develop.
Abstain from alcohol if AATD liver disease is present, as alcohol markedly accelerates hepatic fibrosis.
Genetic counseling before conceiving for PiZZ or PiSZ individuals, as each child of two Z-allele carriers has a 25% risk of PiZZ.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain augmentation therapy infusion schedule consistently; lapses allow serum AAT to fall below the protective 11 µmol/L threshold for neutrophil elastase inhibition.
Participate in structured pulmonary rehabilitation if FEV1 is below 60% predicted or dyspnea limits daily activity.
Stay current with all respiratory vaccines; respiratory infections are a leading cause of exacerbation and lung function decline.
Avoid secondhand smoke exposure — passive smoking inactivates residual AAT through oxidant mechanisms similar to active smoking.
Monitor weight; undernutrition and sarcopenia worsen dyspnea and respiratory muscle weakness in emphysema.
Carry a medical alert card or wear a medical bracelet identifying AATD genotype, especially when traveling internationally.

recommended: Adequate caloric intake to maintain BMI 20-25 kg/m2 — undernutrition independently worsens respiratory muscle strength in COPD/AATD
High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to preserve muscle mass in advanced emphysema and pulmonary cachexia
Fruits and vegetables rich in antioxidants (vitamins C, E, carotenoids) to reduce oxidative inactivation of residual AAT in the lung
Adequate calcium and vitamin D (2,000 IU/day) for patients on inhaled corticosteroids or those with severe emphysema to prevent osteoporosis
avoid: Alcohol in any patient with AATD liver disease — even moderate consumption accelerates hepatic fibrosis in ZZ individuals
Large meals that cause diaphragm compression and worsen breathlessness in severe emphysema; smaller more frequent meals are preferable
High-sodium processed foods in patients with cor pulmonale or peripheral edema from pulmonary hypertension
Antioxidant supplements at pharmacological doses without clinical evidence — high-dose supplemental vitamin E and beta-carotene have shown harm in smoking-related lung disease

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is strongly encouraged in AATD emphysema; it does not accelerate lung damage and significantly improves exercise tolerance, quality of life, and hospitalization rates. A supervised pulmonary rehabilitation program starting at 50-60% of maximum heart rate for 30 minutes, 3-5 times/week, is the target. Patients with resting SpO2 below 88% or exertional desaturation should use supplemental oxygen during exercise to prevent exercise-induced pulmonary hypertension.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed inhalers (LABA, LAMA, ICS) at fixed times daily and carry a short-acting reliever bronchodilator for breakthrough breathlessness.
Monitor oxygen saturation with a home pulse oximeter; seek care if resting SpO2 falls below 90% on two consecutive days.
Complete weekly augmentation therapy infusion as scheduled; contact the infusion provider early if a scheduled dose must be missed.
Recognize exacerbation warning signs (increased breathlessness, change in sputum color, fever) and contact your care team within 24 hours.
Do not smoke; even single-cigarette exposures measurably reduce serum AAT activity for hours through oxidative inactivation.
Schedule annual spirometry, CT emphysema quantification every 2-4 years, and liver function tests every 12 months.
prognosis: Prognosis depends primarily on genotype, smoking status, and the severity of emphysema at diagnosis. PiZZ non-smokers who never develop significant emphysema have near-normal life expectancy. PiZZ smokers have a median survival of approximately 60 years — 15-20 years less than the general population — with FEV1 typically falling below 30% predicted by age 55-65. With augmentation therapy, the CT-measured rate of lung density loss is reduced by approximately one-third in patients with FEV1 35-65% predicted (RAPID Extension Trial, Lancet 2015). Liver disease prognosis is variable: 80-90% of neonatal cholestasis resolves without progression; approximately 3-5% of PiZZ individuals develop cirrhosis severe enough to require liver transplantation by age 50. Post-liver transplant 5-year survival exceeds 80%. Post-lung transplant 5-year survival is 50-60%. Key predictors of poor prognosis are early age of emphysema onset, continued smoking, frequent exacerbations, low BMI, and pulmonary hypertension.
recoveryTimeline: Lung function does not recover with augmentation therapy; treatment goal is preservation of remaining function. FEV1 decline stabilizes toward normal aging rate (25-30 mL/year) within 1-2 years of successful augmentation. Smoking cessation improves FEV1 trajectory within 6-12 months. Liver function recovers within months of transplantation with graft function restored.
complications: Severe panacinar emphysema progressing to respiratory failure (FEV1 below 15-20% predicted) and chronic hypoxic respiratory failure requiring long-term supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation.

Pulmonary hypertension developing in 20-30% of advanced AATD lung disease, worsening dyspnea, right heart failure, and prognosis — detected by echocardiography and confirmed by right heart catheterization.

Hepatic cirrhosis and portal hypertension from Z-protein accumulation in hepatocytes; esophageal varices in 30-40% of cirrhotic AATD patients; hepatocellular carcinoma risk elevated approximately 5-fold over general population.
Spontaneous pneumothorax from emphysematous bulla rupture — occurs in approximately 2-5% of AATD patients over a lifetime; managed with chest drain; recurrence rate above 50% without pleurodesis.
AATD-associated vasculitis (PR3-ANCA and MPO-ANCA positive) causing glomerulonephritis, pulmonary-renal syndrome, or cutaneous vasculitis — requires immunosuppression and AATD-specific awareness.
Panniculitis with painful ulcerating skin lesions that can progress to significant local tissue destruction if not treated with dapsone or colchicine.

supportResources.name: Alpha-1 Foundation
supportResources.description: The leading US patient organization for AATD; provides genetic testing referrals, care center locator, clinical trial registry, and patient education programs.

supportResources.name: Alpha-1 Global (International Patient Registry)
supportResources.description: International registry and advocacy network connecting patients with research and specialist centers worldwide.

supportResources.name: American Lung Association — Alpha-1 Resources
supportResources.description: Plain-language guides on AATD lung disease, augmentation therapy, and smoking cessation support.

confusedWithConditions.name: COPD (non-AATD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Standard COPD from smoking typically causes upper-lobe paraseptal emphysema after age 60. AATD produces basal-predominant panacinar emphysema by age 40-50 in smokers. The key distinction is onset age, distribution on CT, and serum AAT level. Every COPD patient under 45 should have serum AAT measured.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can cause progressive liver fibrosis and cirrhosis. Hepatitis B is distinguished by serological markers (HBsAg, anti-HBc). AATD liver disease requires serum AAT, phenotyping, and liver biopsy showing PAS-D-positive periportal globules. Young patients with unexplained cirrhosis and negative viral hepatitis serology should have AATD excluded.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mesothelioma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both affect the chest cavity in middle-aged adults. Mesothelioma is a malignant pleural tumor from asbestos exposure; AATD causes intraparenchymal emphysema. CT appearance differs completely: pleural thickening and pleural effusion in mesothelioma vs. parenchymal bullae and hyperinflation in AATD.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lung cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both AATD and lung cancer cause progressive breathlessness and weight loss and may coexist. Lung cancer presents with a new mass on imaging; AATD shows diffuse emphysema. AATD may slightly elevate lung cancer risk in smokers, but it is not an established primary lung cancer predisposition.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: AATD is the best-characterized genetic cause of COPD; emphysema management follows GOLD COPD guidelines with the addition of augmentation therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Lung cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Emphysema in general — and smoking in AATD specifically — elevates lung cancer risk; both diseases co-occur and share CT surveillance overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Mesothelioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both affect the lungs in middle age; occupational exposure history links AATD dust/fume risk with asbestos-related mesothelioma in shared occupational populations.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis B
relatedConditions.relevance: Both cause progressive liver fibrosis; co-infection with HBV in a PiZZ patient substantially accelerates cirrhosis and requires vigilance.

faqs.question: What is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency?
faqs.answer: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is a hereditary condition caused by mutations in the SERPINA1 gene that reduce or alter the serum protein alpha-1 antitrypsin. Low levels allow unchecked enzyme activity that destroys lung tissue, causing early emphysema. Misfolded protein trapped in liver cells can cause liver disease.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency?
faqs.answer: Lung symptoms include progressive breathlessness, chronic cough, wheezing, and recurrent chest infections, often appearing by age 40-50 in smokers. Liver symptoms include jaundice, abdominal swelling, and fatigue from cirrhosis. Some patients also develop painful skin nodules (panniculitis) on the trunk.

faqs.question: How is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency inherited?
faqs.answer: AATD is autosomal codominant — both copies of the SERPINA1 gene contribute. Two Z alleles (PiZZ) cause severe deficiency. If both parents carry one Z allele, each child has a 25% chance of PiZZ. About 1 in 2,500 to 5,000 people of Northern European descent carry the PiZZ genotype.

faqs.question: How is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a blood test measuring serum AAT level. If low, phenotyping by isoelectric focusing and genotyping by SERPINA1 PCR confirm the specific alleles. CT chest scan shows basal-predominant emphysema. Liver biopsy with PAS-diastase staining identifies AAT polymer deposits in the liver.

faqs.question: What is augmentation therapy for AATD?
faqs.answer: Augmentation therapy is weekly intravenous infusion of pooled human AAT (Prolastin-C, Zemaira, or similar) to raise blood AAT levels above the protective threshold of 11 micromol/L. It is approved for PiZZ patients with confirmed emphysema and FEV1 30-65% predicted. It slows lung density loss but does not restore lost lung function.

faqs.question: Does alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency only affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: No. The Z-protein misfolds and accumulates in liver cells, causing neonatal cholestasis, liver fibrosis, cirrhosis, and in some patients hepatocellular carcinoma. Skin panniculitis and a form of ANCA vasculitis are also recognized. Lung disease is the most common and serious manifestation in adults.

faqs.question: Can people with AATD smoke?
faqs.answer: Smoking is extremely harmful in AATD. It inactivates residual AAT and directly recruits more white blood cells to the lung. PiZZ smokers develop significant emphysema 10-15 years earlier than PiZZ non-smokers and have a median survival roughly 15-20 years shorter. Cessation at any age slows lung function decline.

faqs.question: Is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency curable?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for the underlying genetic defect. Liver transplantation is curative for liver disease as the donor liver produces normal AAT. Gene therapy and RNA-targeting therapies are in clinical trials. Lung disease is manageable but not reversible; augmentation therapy slows progression.

faqs.question: Who should be tested for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency?
faqs.answer: ATS/ERS guidelines recommend testing all adults with COPD or emphysema, especially those under 45 or without significant smoking history; all patients with unexplained liver disease; and all first-degree relatives of confirmed AATD cases. Testing requires only a blood draw for serum AAT level.

faqs.question: What is the PiZZ genotype in alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency?
faqs.answer: PiZZ means both copies of the SERPINA1 gene carry the Z allele, producing only 15-20% of normal AAT levels. This is the most common severe genotype and the target of augmentation therapy approval. PiMZ and PiSZ are intermediate genotypes with moderately reduced AAT and lower lung disease risk.

faqs.question: How common is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency?
faqs.answer: Severe AATD (PiZZ) occurs in approximately 1 in 2,500 to 1 in 5,000 individuals of Northern European descent, making it one of the most common potentially fatal hereditary disorders in adults. An estimated 85-90% of those affected worldwide remain undiagnosed.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency?
faqs.answer: PiZZ non-smokers who are diagnosed early and never smoke have near-normal life expectancy. PiZZ smokers have a median survival approximately 15-20 years shorter than average. Augmentation therapy slows CT-measured emphysema progression by about one-third. Liver transplant for end-stage liver disease has over 80% five-year survival.

faqs.question: Can children develop alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. PiZZ neonates may develop neonatal cholestasis (jaundice, pale stools) in the first weeks of life; 80-90% resolve spontaneously. A minority progress to childhood cirrhosis. Lung disease is uncommon in children but accelerates rapidly with smoking in adolescence and early adulthood.

faqs.question: Is augmentation therapy available worldwide?
faqs.answer: Augmentation therapy is FDA-approved in the US and approved in Canada and most of Western Europe. It is not widely available in low- and middle-income countries due to cost and supply chain constraints. Cost per year of treatment ranges from approximately $60,000 to $120,000 in the US.

faqs.question: What new treatments are being developed for AATD?
faqs.answer: Several approaches are in clinical trials: inhaled AAT for direct lung delivery, gene therapy using AAV vectors to restore normal SERPINA1 expression in the liver, RNA interference (siRNA) to silence the Z allele and prevent liver polymer accumulation, and small-molecule correctors that help misfolded Z-protein reach its normal conformation.

metaDescription: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency: inherited cause of early emphysema and liver disease. PiZZ genotype, augmentation therapy, and prognosis reviewed by aihealz.
